text: A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, entering the preperitoneal space. A preperitoneal flap is created, and a preperitoneal pocket is formed. The round ligament is visibly separated from the peritoneal flap.

The surgeon clears preperitoneal fat in the Hasselbach triangle and carries the dissection inferiorly to identify the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament. A femoral hernia is identified, and the preperitoneal fat incarcerated into the femoral canal is mobilized and removed.

The surgeon continues dissection inferiorly and laterally, identifying the right indirect inguinal defect. The incarcerated preperitoneal fat is completely reduced, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hernia.
The video shows a large amount of preperitoneal fat that is incarcerated into the left indirect inguinal defect, highlighting the extent of the hernia.

The surgeon continues dissection superiorly to complete the creation of the preperitoneal pocket. A large three-dimensional mesh is inserted into the space to cover both the left indirect inguinal hernia and femoral hernia defects.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the abdominal wall using a TCO. Following this, the peritoneal defect is closed with an absorbable 2-0 V-Loc suture in a simple running fashion.
